article_title: Serial-to-IP Devices Hide Thousands of Old & New Bugs
core_thesis: Serial-to-IP converters, essential for bridging legacy industrial machinery with modern IP networks, are critically insecure: they harbor both newly discovered vulnerabilities (20 in popular models) and thousands of known bugs in their outdated software stacks, making them prime targets for OT attacks. The market for these devices is growing, yet vendors fail to apply basic security hardening, leaving industrial networks exposed.
detailed_summary: Researchers from Forescout presented findings at Black Hat Asia 2026 revealing 20 new vulnerabilities in three popular serial-to-IP converter models from Lantronix (EDS3000PS, EDS5000PS) and Silex (SD330-AC). The EDS5000PS had five RCE vulnerabilities, two critical (CVSS 9.8), and three high-severity requiring authentication. The EDS3000PS had a critical flaw (CVE-2025-70082, CVSS 9.8) allowing password change without old password, enabling takeover and admin lockout. Silex's SD330-AC had 12 new bugs. Beyond new bugs, Forescout analyzed the firmware of these devices and found an average of 212 known vulnerabilities in open-source components and 2,255 bugs in the Linux kernel per device, with 63 critical, 29% high-severity, and 89 publicly available exploits per firmware image. The devices run ancient Linux versions and lack binary hardening like ASLR, making exploitation easier. The researchers demonstrated real-world impact by showing how an attacker could alter data from a barcode reader or thermometer as it passes through the converter. The market for these devices is expected to grow, and they have been used in major OT attacks (e.g., 2015 Ukraine power grid, 2025 Poland attack).

entities.name: Forescout
entities.role: Cybersecurity firm that conducted the vulnerability research and presented at Black Hat Asia 2026.

entities.name: Lantronix
entities.role: Vendor of serial-to-IP converters (EDS3000PS, EDS5000PS) found to have critical vulnerabilities.

entities.name: Silex
entities.role: Vendor of serial-to-IP converter (SD330-AC) found to have 12 new vulnerabilities.

entities.name: Daniel dos Santos
entities.role: Head of security research at Forescout, who previewed the Black Hat demo and commented on the findings.

entities.name: Black Hat Asia 2026
entities.role: Conference where the research was presented.

key_data_points: 20 new vulnerabilities were discovered across three serial-to-IP converter models: 8 in Lantronix EDS3000PS/EDS5000PS and 12 in Silex SD330-AC. (🟢 Red Teaming: these are fresh attack vectors for OT environments.)

The Lantronix EDS5000PS contains five RCE vulnerabilities, two critical (CVSS 9.8) and three high-severity (requiring authentication). (🟢 Red Teaming: critical RCE can be used for initial access or lateral movement in OT networks.)

CVE-2025-70082 in the EDS3000PS allows changing the device password without providing the old password, enabling attacker takeover and admin lockout (CVSS 9.8). (🟢 Red Teaming: simple exploitation path to gain persistent control and disrupt operations.)

On average, each serial-to-IP firmware image contains 212 known vulnerabilities in open-source components and 2,255 bugs in the Linux kernel. (🟢 Red Teaming: vast attack surface; many known exploits can be reused.)

68% of bugs are low/medium severity, 29% high, and 63 are critical. Each firmware image is vulnerable to an average of 89 publicly available exploits. (🟢 Red Teaming: high probability of finding a working exploit for these devices.)

The devices run ancient Linux versions and lack binary hardening techniques like ASLR, making exploitation more reliable. (🟢 Red Teaming: easier to develop and execute exploits; memory corruption attacks are more likely to succeed.)

Forescout estimates over 10 million serial device servers exist globally, with tens of thousands exposed on the open web. (🟢 Red Teaming: large attack surface; internet-facing devices are low-hanging fruit.)

Serial-to-IP converters were used in major OT attacks, including the 2015 Ukraine power grid attack and the 2025 Poland attack, to cut operator-machine communication and delay recovery. (🟢 Red Teaming: proven real-world impact; targeting these devices can cause significant disruption.)
conclusions: Serial-to-IP converters are a critical weak point in OT security due to their outdated software stacks and lack of hardening, making them attractive targets for attackers. The discovery of 20 new vulnerabilities and thousands of known bugs underscores the urgent need for patching and security improvements.

The market for these devices is growing, not shrinking, meaning the risk will persist and likely increase. Organizations must prioritize securing these devices, but patching is difficult in always-on industrial environments.

Binary hardening techniques like ASLR are not widely implemented, leaving devices vulnerable to reliable exploitation. Vendors need to adopt modern security practices, and operators should consider network segmentation and monitoring to mitigate risks.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your Red Teaming and cybersecurity interests, as it details specific vulnerabilities in OT devices that can be exploited for offensive operations. The critical RCE bugs and password bypass (CVE-2025-70082) provide concrete attack vectors for gaining initial access or causing disruption in industrial networks. The finding that thousands of known vulnerabilities exist in these devices means you can leverage existing exploits rather than developing new ones. As a programmer, you could write custom payloads or scripts to automate exploitation of these devices. The article also highlights the importance of binary hardening—or lack thereof—which you can test in your own homelab to practice exploit development. For your studies, the research methodology (vulnerability discovery, firmware analysis) offers a model for security research projects.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a homelab with a serial-to-IP converter (e.g., Lantronix EDS3000PS) to practice exploiting the password bypass (CVE-2025-70082) and RCE vulnerabilities. This will give you hands-on experience with OT exploitation, which is a valuable skill for Red Teaming. Additionally, you could write a tool to scan for internet-exposed serial device servers and test for known vulnerabilities, contributing to your portfolio.
